Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement. Avoid swinging your back to lift the weight. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Dumbbell Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement, biceps tear

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with palms facing forward. Keep elbows close to your sides.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders, contracting your biceps. Keep your upper arms stationary.
Pause briefly at the top, squeezing your biceps. Do not let your shoulders roll forward.
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with control. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
If you can't keep your elbows still, the weight is too heavy. Focus on the negative (lowering) phase for better growth.

common_mistakes.mistake: Swinging the back to generate momentum.
Moving the elbows forward during the curl.
Using wrists to curl instead of biceps.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.

Pose.safety: Keep your wrists neutral (straight) throughout the movement. Avoid rotating your palms—the hammer grip is fixed. Control the movement.
Pose.Name: Hammer Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, forearm strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides with palms facing each other (neutral grip). Keep elbows pinned to your sides.
Exhale as you curl the dumbbells toward your shoulders, maintaining the neutral palm position throughout.
Pause at the top, feeling the contraction in your brachialis (outer forearm).
Inhale as you lower with control to the starting position. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
This exercise builds thickness in the upper arm. Keep your wrists straight—don't let them bend backward or forward.

common_mistakes.mistake: Rotating palms upward during the curl.
Letting elbows flare out from the body.
Using shoulder momentum to lift.
Wrist flexion instead of maintaining neutral.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. Avoid letting your elbows flare outward. Control the weight on both the push and the return.
Pose.Name: Tricep Pushdown (Cable/Band)
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine or Resistance Band, Rope or Bar Attachment
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 heads - Long, Lateral, Medial)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a rope or straight bar to a high pulley. Grip the attachment with palms facing down, elbows at 90 degrees, tucked to sides.
Exhale as you extend your arms downward until they are straight, keeping elbows stationary.
Pause briefly at the bottom, squeezing your triceps.
Inhale as you slowly return to the starting position with control. Perform 2 sets of 10 reps.
Only your forearms should move—your upper arms remain vertical and still throughout the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward or move forward.
Using body weight to lean into the push.
Rushing the return phase (eccentric).
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders down and back. Lower yourself only as far as your shoulders comfortably allow. Avoid letting your elbows flare outward.
Pose.Name: Bench Dip
Pose.Equipment: Bench or Chair, Optional: Weight on Lap
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injury, wrist injury, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on the edge of a bench. Place hands shoulder-width apart on the bench, fingers forward. Walk feet forward, supporting your weight with straight arms.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body by bending your elbows to 90 degrees. Keep back close to the bench.
Exhale as you push through your palms to straighten your arms, returning to start.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep shoulders down and away from ears.
The closer your feet are to your body, the easier the dip. For more challenge, extend legs further forward or add weight to your lap.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders hunch up toward the ears.
Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees) causing shoulder strain.
Elbows flaring outward instead of tracking back.
Using leg assistance to push up.

Pose.safety: Keep your core engaged and back straight. Avoid arching your lower back. Do not lock out your elbows at the top.
Pose.Name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, AC joint injury, neck strain

steps.step: Sit on a bench with back support. Hold dumbbells at shoulder height, palms facing forward, elbows bent at 90 degrees.
Exhale as you press the dumbbells directly overhead until arms are extended (but not locked).
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale as you lower with control back to shoulder height.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core braced to protect your lower back.
Do not let the dumbbells touch at the top—keep them shoulder-width apart to maintain tension on the deltoids.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back to help lift the weight.
Bringing dumbbells too close together at the top.
Flaring elbows outward excessively.
Using momentum instead of controlled pressing.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement. Avoid using momentum to swing the weights up. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, palms facing your body. Slight bend in elbows, shoulders down and back.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Stop when arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly, then inhale as you lower with control back to starting position.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Do not let your shoulders shrug up toward your ears.
Use lighter weight than you think you need. The medial deltoid is a small muscle that responds best to higher reps and strict form.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights up.
Raising arms above shoulder height.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs for stability. Move only your wrists—keep your arms and body still. Avoid jerky movements.
Pose.Name: Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, carpal tunnel syndrome, elbow tendinitis

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over your knees, palms facing up. Hold a barbell or dumbbells.
Exhale as you curl your wrists upward, bringing the weight toward your forearms.
Pause at the top, squeezing your forearm flexors.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
Focus on full range of motion. Let your wrists extend fully at the bottom before curling back up.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the arms instead of isolating the wrists.
Using momentum to swing the weight.
Letting wrists hyperextend at the bottom.
Holding the breath during the curl.

Pose.safety: Keep your forearms fully supported. Move only your wrists. Avoid jerking the weight—control is key.
Pose.Name: Reverse Wrist Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Wrist tendinitis, tennis elbow, carpal tunnel syndrome

steps.step: Sit on a bench. Rest your forearms on your thighs with wrists hanging over knees, palms facing down. Hold a barbell or dumbbells.
Exhale as you extend your wrists upward, raising the back of your hands toward the ceiling.
Pause at the top, feeling the contraction in your forearm extensors.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
This exercise is often harder than regular wrist curls. Start with very light weight—even just the empty bar—to master the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Moving the entire arm instead of just the wrist.
Using too much weight, causing jerky movement.
Letting wrists drop too low at the bottom.
Failing to control the descent.

Pose.safety: Avoid using your lower back to swing the weight. Keep your core braced and elbows pinned to your sides. Control the barbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Barbell Curl
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, lower back strain, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Stand holding a barbell with an underhand grip (palms up), hands shoulder-width apart. Elbows pinned to sides.
Exhale as you curl the barbell toward your shoulders, contracting your biceps. Keep upper arms stationary.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps hard.
Inhale as you lower with control to starting position. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
A wider grip targets the inner biceps (short head); a narrower grip targets the outer biceps (long head). Experiment to find what works for you.

common_mistakes.mistake: Swinging the back to lift the weight (cheating curl).
Letting elbows drift forward.
Using wrist flexion instead of elbow flexion.
Dropping the weight quickly on the descent.

Pose.safety: Keep your shoulders and upper back pressed firmly against the bench. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells.
Pose.Name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Biceps Brachii (Long Head - Outer Biceps), Brachialis
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, shoulder impingement, lower back issues

steps.step: Set an adjustable bench to 45-60 degrees. Lie back with a dumbbell in each hand, arms hanging straight down, palms facing forward.
Exhale as you curl both dumbbells toward your shoulders, keeping elbows stationary and back against the bench.
Pause at the top, squeezing your biceps.
Inhale as you lower with control to the starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
The incline position stretches the biceps more than standing curls, which increases muscle activation. Don't let your shoulders roll forward.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting shoulders lift off the bench.
Using momentum to swing the dumbbells up.
Elbows moving forward during the curl.
Lowering arms too quickly.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pointing toward the ceiling throughout. Avoid flaring elbows outward. Control the weight on the descent.
Pose.Name: Skull Crushers (Lying Tricep Extension)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 Heads, especially Long Head)
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Lie on a bench holding an EZ-bar with an overhand grip. Extend arms straight up over your chest, shoulder-width apart.
Inhale as you bend your elbows, lowering the bar toward your forehead (or behind your head). Keep elbows pointing up.
Exhale as you extend your arms back to the starting position, squeezing your triceps.
Perform 3 sets of 10 reps. Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout.
The name sounds scary, but you're lowering to your forehead—not through it! Stop when your forearms are parallel to the floor.

common_mistakes.mistake: Letting elbows flare outward.
Lowering the bar past your head (too deep).
Arching the lower back to help lift.
Using momentum to extend the arms.

Pose.safety: Keep your elbows pinned to your sides. Avoid leaning forward or using body momentum. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Cable Pushdown
Pose.Equipment: Cable Machine with V-Bar or Rope
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Elbow tendinitis, wrist injury, shoulder impingement

steps.step: Attach a V-bar or rope to a high pulley. Grip with palms facing down. Stand close to the cable, elbows at 90 degrees and tucked to sides.
Exhale as you push the attachment down until your arms are fully extended.
If using a rope, split your hands apart at the bottom for a better contraction.
Inhale as you slowly return to the starting position. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
At the bottom of the movement, think about pushing your palms toward the floor to fully contract the triceps.

common_mistakes.mistake: Leaning forward and using body weight to push.
Letting elbows flare away from the body.
Rushing the return (eccentric) phase.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.

Pose.safety: Lower yourself only as far as your shoulders comfortably allow. Keep your body close to the bars. Avoid letting your shoulders roll forward.
Pose.Name: Tricep Dips
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Triceps Brachii (All 3 Heads), Chest (Minor), Anterior Deltoid
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff injury, elbow tendinitis, wrist injury

steps.step: Grip parallel bars with palms facing inward. Jump or step up to support your weight with arms straight, shoulders down.
Inhale as you slowly lower your body by bending your elbows to 90 degrees. Keep torso upright (lean forward for chest focus, upright for triceps).
Exhale as you push through your palms to straighten your arms, returning to start.
Perform 2 sets of 10 reps. Keep your core engaged throughout.
For triceps focus, keep your body as upright as possible. If you can't do 10 reps, use an assisted dip machine or resistance bands for support.

common_mistakes.mistake: Lowering too deep (past 90 degrees), straining shoulders.
Letting shoulders roll forward and hunch.
Using momentum to push up.
Failing to keep core engaged.

Pose.safety: Keep your core braced and lower back neutral. Avoid arching your back to press heavier weight. Control the barbell throughout.
Pose.Name: Overhead Press (Barbell)
Pose.Equipment: Barbell with Weight Plates, Squat Rack (Optional)
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Anterior and Medial Heads), Triceps, Trapezius, Core Stabilizers
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Shoulder impingement, rotator cuff tear, lower back injury, neck strain

steps.step: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart. Hold the barbell at your collarbone, hands slightly wider than shoulders, palms forward. Elbows slightly in front of the bar.
Exhale as you press the barbell directly overhead, moving your head slightly back to clear the path. Keep core braced.
At the top, the bar should be directly over your shoulders and ears. Do not hyperextend your lower back.
Inhale as you lower with control back to collarbone. Perform 3 sets of 10 reps.
Squeeze your glutes and brace your core throughout the press. This protects your lower back and gives you a stable base.

common_mistakes.mistake: Arching the lower back excessively (leaning back).
Moving the bar around the head instead of moving the head back.
Locking the elbows hyperextended at the top.
Letting the bar drift forward of the shoulders.

Pose.safety: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells. Control the descent.
Pose.Name: Lateral Raise
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, neck strain

steps.step: Stand holding dumbbells at your sides, palms facing your body. Slight bend in elbows, shoulders down.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Stop when arms are parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly, then inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 3 sets of 12 reps.
At the top, imagine pouring water from a pitcher—slightly rotate your thumbs down for better medial delt activation.
Don't let your shoulders shrug up. Keep them down and back throughout the movement.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the weights up.
Raising arms above shoulder height.
Shrugging shoulders toward the ears.
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Keep your chest supported against the bench. Avoid using momentum to swing the dumbbells. Control the movement throughout.
Pose.Name: Rear Delt Fly
Pose.Focused_Muscles: Deltoids (Posterior Head - Rear Shoulder), Rhomboids, Trapezius
Pose.Not_For_Injuries: Rotator cuff injury, shoulder impingement, lower back strain

steps.step: Set an incline bench to 30-45 degrees. Lie face down on the bench (chest supported) holding dumbbells with palms facing each other.
Exhale as you raise the dumbbells out to your sides, leading with your elbows. Keep a slight bend in your elbows.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together. Your arms should be parallel to the floor.
Inhale as you lower with control back to starting position. Perform 2 sets of 12 reps.
Focus on moving from your shoulders, not your arms. Imagine squeezing a pencil between your shoulder blades at the top.

common_mistakes.mistake: Using momentum to swing the dumbbells.
Rounding the upper back instead of squeezing the shoulder blades.
Raising arms too high (past parallel).
Locking elbows straight.

Pose.safety: Rest your forearms fully on your thighs. Move only your wrists. Control the weight throughout.
Pose.Name: Barbell Wrist Curl
